exemplar.title: In what ways do the screenwriter and director use language and elements of mise en scene to emphasise and critique the constraints imposed by gender roles in the 2005 film "Pride and Prejudice"?

detailedExemplarData.title: In what ways do the screenwriter and director use language and elements of mise en scene to emphasise and critique the constraints imposed by gender roles in the 2005 film "Pride and Prejudice"?

subject.title: English Lang & Lit (Old)
subject.description: IB English Language & Literature combines the study of language and literature, analyzing both literary and non-literary texts across various media and contexts. Students explore how language shapes meaning, identity, and culture through critical examination of diverse text types including advertisements, speeches, novels, and digital media. The course develops analytical skills, cultural understanding, and critical thinking about communication in different contexts and purposes.

strengths: The research question is explicitly stated, focused and consistently referred to throughout the introduction and early analysis.
Sections are outlined clearly with a coherent background that situates the film and thematic focus.
Methodology demonstrates effective selection of key scenes (e.g., the hand-touch sequence) and a range of relevant analytical approaches (dialogue, camera angles, mise en scÃ¨ne).
weaknesses: Some phrasing in the introduction remains vague and at times repetitive, diluting analytical precision.
Occasional drift into plot summary weakens the direct connection between methodology and research question.
In-text citation is inconsistent, indicating partial control over referencing conventions.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work communicates its topic with clarity of purpose, offers a sharply defined research question, and employs a generally complete methodology. While there are lapses in analytical framing and referencing, the overall focus, question articulation and methodological planning sit at the stronger end of Band 3â4, warranting a score of 4.

strengths: Demonstrates solid familiarity with narrative, characters and cinematic techniques through accurate scene descriptions and dialogue quotations.
Terminology (e.g. mise en scÃ¨ne, male gaze, pathetic fallacy) is used consistently and appropriately throughout.
Shows contextual understanding of Regency gender norms aligned with film examples.
weaknesses: Heavy reliance on informal and popular web sources limits scholarly depth.
Some assertions (e.g. about patriarchy) remain under-substantiated by peer-reviewed or authoritative materials.
Occasional conceptual slips (e.g. oversimplifying âmale gazeâ) reduce analytical precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student exhibits clear, coherent knowledge of the topic and generally accurate, consistent use of subject-specific terminology. However, the uneven quality of sources and occasional conceptual imprecision constrain the work to the mid-band for Knowledge and Understanding, justifying a score of 3.

strengths: Primary research (scene analyses) is directly relevant to the research question and shows thoughtful textual engagement.
Argument structure uses thematic headings effectively, creating a discernible line of development.
Select instances of insightful analysis (e.g. gesture linking to social norms) elevate moments of critical depth.
weaknesses: Secondary research relies heavily on non-scholarly sources, weakening sustained academic credibility.
Analysis often remains descriptive or speculative without explicit linkage back to the gender-constraint critique.
Critical evaluation is surface-level, with few counter-arguments or synthesized interpretations in the conclusion.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation moves beyond adequacy into the âgoodâ range: research is largely relevant, analysis contains supported and occasionally insightful observations, and a coherent argument thread persists throughout. Despite reliance on informal sources and limited counter-argumentation, the overall quality aligns with the lower half of Band 7â9, warranting 7 marks.

strengths: Includes all required elements: title page with word count, table of contents, section headings and works-cited list.
Paragraphing and headings are clear and facilitate navigation.
Bibliography is alphabetised; italics and indentation for quotations are correctly applied.
weaknesses: Table of contents page numbers do not always align with actual sections, causing minor navigation issues.
Inconsistent hyperlink formatting and occasional line-spacing lapses interrupt visual consistency.
Pagination markers appear unclear in places, slightly hindering reader flow.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The structure and formatting meet core IB presentation requirements and largely apply layout conventions correctly. Minor inconsistencies in pagination and hyperlink styling place this work at the lower end of Band 3â4, justifying a score of 3.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The essay draws on close reading of the film, the published screenplay, and a list of secondary web articles and essays.  Methodological choicesâscene selection, dialogue analysis, reference to camera workâare appropriate to a Category 3 English EE.

However, the range of sources leans heavily on popular reviews, blogs and basic study websites; academic film theory or authoritative Austen scholarship is limited.  In-text citation is inconsistent and occasionally absent, indicating only partial evidence of informed selection and referencing control.

Overall the methodology can be called âmostly completeâ but not consistently effective, placing it in Band 3-4 with a mid-band score of 3.

Formatting conventions such as word count declaration, section headings, use of italics for film titles, and an alphabetised bibliography are present.  Citations follow a recognisable (though inconsistent) parenthetical style and long quotations are indented.

There are lapses: inconsistent hyperlink formatting, occasional missing line-spacing, and unclear pagination markers.  Nonetheless, the majority of required layout considerations are correctly applied.

Therefore the work meets Band 3-4 expectations; a score of 3 is assigned.

Primary research (detailed scene analysis) is strong and unquestionably relevant to the research question.  Secondary research spans c. 15 sources, showing some effort to triangulate views on gender, film technique, and Regency society.

Nevertheless, many sources are popular rather than scholarly, and credibility is uneven.  Citations appear but are not always integrated or critically interrogated, so relevance is partial rather than sustained.

These characteristics align with the middle of Band 4-6 (adequate).  A score of 5 reflects competent but not consistently rigorous research relevance.

The introduction states a clear research focus on the 2005 film version of Pride and Prejudice and signals that gender-role constraints will be examined through language and mise en scÃ¨ne.  The student outlines the sections that will follow and provides a concise background to the film, so the reader understands what is being analysed.

Although the purpose is understandable, phrasing is sometimes repetitive and occasionally vague (e.g.
 âThis essay will discuss gender rolesâ¦â without specifying precise analytical aims).  Nonetheless, the topic itself is never in doubt and the reader can follow the line of inquiry without difficulty.

Because communication is generally clear but not highly sophisticated or sharply delimited, the strand best aligns with Band 3-4; a mid-band score of 3 is appropriate.

An overarching argument is present: the film both reinforces and critiques 19th-century gender constraints.  The student structures the discussion around thematic headings (marriage, relationship, societal expectations), enabling a line of development.

However, evaluation remains surface-level.  There is little weighing of differing scholarly interpretations, and conclusions often restate points rather than synthesise or question their implications.  Some sections digress into lengthy narrative recount.

Consequently the work meets the "adequate" descriptor, warranting a score of 4 within Band 4-6.

The student moves beyond plot summary by linking dialogue, camera work and staging to gender themes, offering some insightful observations (e.g. disappearance of ballroom dancers to foreground DarcyâElizabeth tension).  Paragraphs generally contain topic sentences and supporting evidence.

Yet analysis is often descriptive or speculative (âas if he felt consumed by her presenceâ), and claims about patriarchy or the male gaze are sometimes asserted rather than argued with evidence.  Counter-arguments and alternative readings are rare, reducing analytical depth.

Thus the work sits at the lower end of the adequate 4-6 band; a score of 4 best captures the mixed analytical effectiveness.

The essay includes a title page with word count, a table of contents, titled sections, and a works-cited list, fulfilling core structural requirements.  Paragraphing and headings allow the reader to navigate the argument.

Minor issues exist (e.g. some headings do not match ToC page numbers exactly, page divisions noted as {0}, {1} etc.), yet overall the structure is clearly appropriate and matches presentation guidelines.

These features correspond to the lower half of Band 3-4; a score of 3 is merited.

The student demonstrates solid familiarity with the filmâs narrative, characters, and key scenes, quoting dialogue accurately and describing cinematic techniques such as shot-reverse-shot, close-ups and pathetic fallacy.  Contextual understanding of Regency gender norms is shown, though sometimes supported by superficial sources.

Use of secondary material is uneven: while the bibliography is lengthy, many references are to informal websites; scholarly depth is therefore limited and some assertions remain unsubstantiated.

Because knowledge is generally clear but not exemplary or fully synthesised with high-quality sources, Band 3-4 is most fitting; a score of 3 recognises the good but not excellent level.

Film-studies and literary terminologyâmise en scÃ¨ne, male gaze, pathetic fallacy, camera angles, Regency, patriarchyâappears regularly and is mostly accurate.  Occasional slips occur (e.g. confusing "male gaze" as purely objectifying without nuance), yet general conceptual control is evident.

Key concepts such as class, ideology and gender dynamics are applied in a basically correct manner, even if analytical precision fluctuates.

Given the largely accurate but not consistently sophisticated use of subject terms, Band 3-4 is appropriate; a mid-band mark of 3 is awarded.

The research questionââIn what ways do the screenwriter and director use language and elements of mise en scÃ¨ne to emphasise and critique the constraints imposed by gender rolesâ¦?ââis explicitly stated on the title page.  It narrows the investigation to two filmmaking techniques (dialogue and mise en scÃ¨ne) and a single thematic concern (gender constraints) within one identified film.

Most of the subsequent discussion does connect to that question: extended scene analysis, camera-angle commentary, and dialogue quotations are used to probe gender expectations.  Some sections drift into broad plot retelling (especially the lengthy marriage discussion), so the connection is not always tight.

Given the clear phrasing of the RQ and its mostly consistent application, the work matches Band 3-4, and the stronger end of that band (score 4) is justified.

annotations.comment: Cleary stated investigation title and research question immediately orient the reader, showcasing strong adherence to report conventions.
Table of contents entries appear correctly structured but page numbers in ToC donât align with actual sections, causing navigation confusion.
Introduction provides necessary background but occasionally repeats vague phrases (e.g., âThis essay will discuss gender rolesâ¦â), diluting analytical precision.
Refine thesis wording to specify exact analytical aims rather than a general discussion of âgender rolesâ to strengthen focus.
Frequent reliance on informal blogs and study-site sources limits scholarly depth; some claims about Regency norms lack authoritative support.
Incorporate peer-reviewed film theory or Austen scholarshipâsuch as academic journals on mise en scÃ¨neâto bolster methodology and interpretation.
Analysis often recounts scenes descriptively without explicit argument linking filmic detail to gender critique, weakening critical depth.
Introduce and address counter-arguments or alternative readings (e.g., how some critics view the film reinforcing norms) to deepen evaluation.
Effective reference to "pathetic fallacy" demonstrates accurate use of film terminology to explore cinematic symbolism.
Camera-angle analysis notes key moments but stops short of connecting these choices directly to the critique of gender power structures.
Provide a more nuanced definition of the "male gaze" conceptâperhaps referencing Laura Mulveyâto avoid oversimplification.
Some assertions about quick cuts and emotional tension lack textual evidence; link each claim back to specific frames or dialogue.
Selection of intimate hand-touch scene is insightful and demonstrates purposeful methodology in linking gesture to social norms.
Consider how broader historical contextâsuch as Regency courtship customsâfurther informs ungloved hand symbolism in this scene.
Discussion sections rely heavily on popular web sources without critiquing their authority, reducing research credibility.
Critically evaluate the reliability of blog and review sources versus peer-reviewed articles when integrating them into your argument.
Conclusion reiterates main argument but offers little new insight or synthesis of findings.
Extend the conclusion by discussing implications of the filmâs gender critique for contemporary audiences or scholarship.
Character-development section contains extensive plot summary that overshadows analytical argumentation.
Integrate scholarly viewpoints or theoretical frameworks (e.g., Austen criticism) when analysing Elizabethâs development to ground assertions.
Consistent and accurate use of terms such as âmise en scÃ¨ne,â âpatriarchy,â and âmale gazeâ demonstrates strong conceptual control.
The Agnew source lacks a corresponding in-text citation, creating a gap in source attribution.
Analysis of methodology is implicit in discussion but lacks explicit reflection on planning or source selection choices.
Include a brief reflective section on research design choices and challenges to demonstrate evaluative engagement with methodology.
Worksâcited list displays varied citation formats (journal articles, websites) inconsistently, detracting from professional presentation.
Standardise citation formattingâchoose a recognized style (e.g., MLA or Chicago) and apply it consistently across all entries.
